main_summary.summary: Clarence B. Jones, attorney and adviser to Martin Luther King Jr., was interviewed on April 15-16, 2013, in Palo Alto, California, by David Cline with videographer John Bishop for the Civil Rights History Project. Born January 1931 in Philadelphia to domestic-servant parents employed by the J.B. Lippincott family, Jones was placed at age six in a Catholic boarding school for indigent Negro and Native American boys run by the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament near Cornwells Heights, Pennsylvania. He graduated from Palmyra High School in New Jersey, attended Columbia College on financial aid, and was drafted into the Army during the Korean Conflict. After refusing to sign the loyalty oath and citing constitutional objections, he was given an undesirable discharge in 1955 as a security risk; ACLU attorney Stanley Faulkner won an upgrade to honorable discharge on appeal. Jones attended Boston University Law School on the Korean Conflict GI Bill, married Ann Norton of the W.W. Norton publishing family, and moved to California. In February 1960, Judge Hubert T. Delany asked him to clerk on King's Alabama tax-perjury defense; King visited Jones's Altadena home and preached the next day at a Los Angeles church, recruiting Jones from the pulpit. Jones recounts his role drafting opening paragraphs of the I Have a Dream speech at the Willard Hotel and witnessing King's extemporaneous delivery at the March on Washington.
key_themes: Catholic boarding school upbringing and Irish nuns
Korean Conflict draft resistance and military discharge appeal
Recruitment by Martin Luther King Jr.
I Have a Dream speech drafting and delivery
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jones provides a detailed firsthand account of how the opening paragraphs of King's August 28, 1963 March on Washington address were drafted from prior conversations with him and Stanley Levison, and how King delivered the first six or seven prepared paragraphs verbatim before moving the written text aside and continuing extemporaneously after Mahalia Jackson called out for the dream. He documents the internal organizational politics in which conservative clergy on the march committee proposed eight-minute speaking limits with King in the middle of the program, and how A. Philip Randolph backed Jones's argument that King should close.

The interview also preserves the FBI-driven June 22, 1963 conversation in which President Kennedy directly told King to sever ties with Stanley Levison and Jack O'Dell on alleged Communist Party grounds, and the operational workaround in which Jones became the conduit so King and Levison could continue collaborating. Jones's Army court-martial record under McCarthy-era loyalty oath requirements, including his refusal to sign the attorney general's list of subversive organizations and his subsequent reinstatement to honorable discharge, documents the intersection of Cold War security apparatus with early civil rights legal work.

chapters.title: Birth and Catholic Boarding School
chapters.summary: Jones describes being born in 1931 in Philadelphia to domestic-servant parents employed by the J.B. Lippincott family. From age six until nearly fourteen, he was placed at a Catholic boarding school for indigent Negro and Native American boys run by the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament. He recounts the Irish nuns' discipline, the Latin instruction that exempted him from the requirement in high school, and developing a serious stutter during this period.
related_events: Placement at Catholic boarding school for indigent Negro and Native American boys

chapters.title: Palmyra High School and Columbia
chapters.summary: Jones attended Palmyra High School in Palmyra, New Jersey, graduating in 1949 as most likely to succeed. Teachers paid his college application fees out of pocket. He attended Columbia College on a scholarship requiring a B-minus average, taking classes from Nobel laureates Linus Pauling and Polykarp Kusch.

chapters.title: Mother's Death and Clarinet
chapters.summary: While taking midterm exams in January 1952, Jones learned that his mother had been operated on for terminal abdominal-rectal cancer. He set up a study desk at the foot of her bed and finished the semester. She died May 4, 1952, leaving a handwritten letter under her mattress. Jones also recounts his parallel life as a clarinetist, attending Juilliard summer programs and meeting Charlie Parker at Manny's Music in Manhattan.
related_events: Charlie Parker encounter at Manny's Music

chapters.title: Paul Robeson and Political Awakening
chapters.summary: Jones describes meeting Paul Robeson through his uncle, who had acted with Robeson on Broadway. Robeson's televised confrontation with Senator James Eastland before the House Un-American Activities Committee, in which he challenged Eastland's constitutional authority based on Mississippi's exclusion of Black voters, influenced Jones's political consciousness. He recounts Robeson's lesson on the racial significance of a touchdown by a Negro player at Baker's Field.

chapters.title: Draft and Loyalty Oath Refusal
chapters.summary: After his student deferment lapsed in 1953, Jones was drafted at Mt. Holly, New Jersey. The night before induction he wrote a handwritten statement citing the First, Fifth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments, stating he would not serve while denied first-class citizenship. He refused to raise his hand at induction and refused to sign the attorney general's loyalty oath listing 400 subversive organizations. He was assigned to Fort Dix Company D, 47th Regiment.

chapters.title: Undesirable Discharge and Appeal
chapters.summary: After 21 months at Fort Dix as a soldier of the month and good-conduct-medal recipient, Jones was charged as a security risk on allegations including the unsigned loyalty oath and friendships with leftists. A board hearing issued an undesirable discharge August 14, 1955. ACLU attorney Stanley Faulkner filed a writ of mandamus and won an honorable discharge through the Armed Services Discharge Review Board at the Pentagon, qualifying Jones for the Korean Conflict GI Bill.
related_events: Pentagon Armed Services Discharge Review Board appeal

chapters.title: Marriage to Ann Norton
chapters.summary: Jones recounts meeting Ann Norton, daughter of the W.W. Norton publishing family, at the New York Public Library shortly after his discharge. They married, and his stepfather-in-law Daniel H. Crena de Iongh, a Dutch banker and early World Bank treasurer, was personally close to South African apartheid architect Hendrik Verwoerd. Jones describes the eventual reconciliation through chamber music when his mother-in-law heard him play the Mozart clarinet concerto.
related_events: Chamber music sessions in Wilton, Connecticut

chapters.title: Boston University Law and Entertainment Practice
chapters.summary: At Boston University Law School, Jones rented an apartment at 857 Beacon Street through Irish-American superintendent goodwill rooted in his boarding-school nun upbringing. His copyright-law paper won honorable mention in the Nathan Burkan Memorial Copyright Competition, leading professor Donald Shapiro to refer him to Leslie Sharlow, general counsel of Revue Productions in California, where Jones briefly worked alongside Bobby Darin and Sandy Dennis before Sharlow's sudden death.

chapters.title: Recruitment by King
chapters.summary: In February 1960, Judge Hubert T. Delany asked Jones to clerk on King's Alabama state perjury defense. Jones declined due to family obligations. King flew to Los Angeles for a World Affairs Council engagement and visited Jones's Altadena home with Bernard Lee. The next day at a Los Angeles church, King preached about the responsibility of Negro professionals, weaving Jones's biography and the Langston Hughes poem Mother to Son into his sermon. Jones approached the pulpit and asked when to leave for Montgomery.
related_events: Jones's recruitment to King's legal team

chapters.title: March on Washington Planning
chapters.summary: Jones recounts the post-Birmingham wave of more than 1,300 demonstrations across 200 cities and 36 states between May and August 1963. While King was advised against vacationing in the Bahamas or Jamaica, Stanley Levison and Bayard Rustin arranged for King's family to stay at Jones's home. The June 22, 1963 White House meeting in which President Kennedy demanded King sever ties with Levison and Jack O'Dell on FBI Communist Party allegations led to Jones becoming the operational conduit between King and Levison.

chapters.title: I Have a Dream Speech
chapters.summary: Jones describes drafting opening paragraphs at the Willard Hotel, including the promissory note metaphor inspired by raising bail money at Chase Manhattan Bank. King delivered the first six or seven paragraphs verbatim, then moved the written text aside when Mahalia Jackson called out for the dream and continued extemporaneously. Jones characterizes the closing as completely spontaneous except for the Free at Last spiritual quotation. He had argued before the march committee that conservative clergy should not impose eight-minute speaking limits or place King in the middle of the program; A. Philip Randolph backed his argument.

chapters.title: Reflections on King's Legacy
chapters.summary: Jones interprets King's political genius as summoning the conscience of the 88 percent majority to recognize the moral contradiction in denying equal protection to the 12 percent. He extends the analysis to the 2013 commemoration of the I Have a Dream speech, the Newtown school shooting, the Memphis sanitation workers' strike, Beyond Vietnam, Occupy Wall Street, the Omega Boys Club work of Dr. Joseph Marshall Jr., and the question of what King would have said about same-sex marriage.
